=== More questionable overview === In the [[Kojiki]], an ancient Japanese text, [[Emperor Jimmu]] sent King Nikko-za to the Land of Dannami to slay Ku-ga-mimi Mikasa. However, this event is not mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
The Tango Fudoki Zanboku records the story of Rikumimikasa Kugamimikasa, an earth spider that was said to have been present in the Aoba Mountains during the reign of Emperor Gomajo-no-Irihiko. The people were disobedient to the emperor and King Hinokoza was sent with an imperial edict to quell the rebellion. As a result, a rocky rock resembling a golden armor was found and was called the Shogun's armor rock, also known as Narusei. The mountain Aobayama was also called Umamibajiyama, which means "horse ears" in Japanese, due to its two peaks that resemble horse ears. The two peaks have a deity named after kasa, namely [[Rokusho Shrine, Okazaki|Rokusho Shrine]] and [[Aoba Shrine]].<ref name=":0" /> The name Misukitomimikenhiko is from the Kibitakehiko of the Ki era and has meaning "noontomo" in Emperor Sotoku's stage. The emperor also frequently decreed to Yamatokenomikoto to turn the Arau Ryujin and the Maduro Hanunin of the Twelve Provinces of the East to him and make peace with them.<ref name=":0" />
The [[Fudoki]] records the story of [[Emperor Keiko]]'s accusation against Oomimi and Tarumimi of Kochinoshima. He cursed the two, whom he had never met, saying, "Even if they are put to death, it will not be enough to make up for their sins."<ref name=":0" />
== Mimi and Mi as regional chiefs == In " [[Kojiki]] " and " [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]], [[Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|" Suetsumimi]] is in [[Izumi Province|the Izumi region]], {{Lang|zh|橛}} is in [[Settsu Province|the Settsu region]], Kugamimi is in [[Tamba Province|the Tamba region]], and Maetsu is in [[Tajima Province|the Tajima region]] . An ear (Masaki tsumimi) has been recorded, but both are believed to be chiefs of their localities. In " [[Amatsuhikone|Izumo]] {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|lt=no Kuni Fudoki|de}} ", people with "mi" such as Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned, all of whom are regional chiefs. <ref>溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref>
In addition, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters ) ({{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
In addition, Ikatomi of [[Ōmi Province|Ika]] [[Ika District, Shiga|County]], Omi Province (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ) is also thought to represent a regional chief with the addition of mi to the place name. The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style posthumous name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .
== Mimi and Mi at shrines == [[彌美神社|Mimi Shrine]] in [[Wakasa Province]], [[Suwa-taisha|Minakatatomi Shrine]] in [[Shinano Province]], {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in Omi Province, [[加夜奈留美神社|Kayanarumi Shrine]] in [[Ōmi Province|Takaichi]] County, Yamato Province, [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] [[波爾移麻比彌神社|Haniyamahimi-jinja Shrine]] in [[Mima District, Tokushima|Mima]] -gun is a shrine that has Mimi and Mi in its name.
== Mimi and Mi in surname == Representative examples of [[Uji (clan)|clan]] names ending in Mi include
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Examples of clan names that do not have mi but have mi in their [[Kamo clan|ancestors]] are
* {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kamo clan; * Deshichio's ancestor Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} 's ancestor Misuki-tomo-mimi, * [[紀氏 (神別)|Ki-shi's]] ( {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} ) ancestor Toyomimi-no-mikoto, and Hikun (Hi-kun) ), * the ancestor of the [[日置部氏|Hiokibe]] clan, Ame no Kushimi, * and the ancestor of the {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}}, Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} == Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amanoshinomimi]], as a child of [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] (also known as Hohodemi),
* [[Kamuyaimimi|Tagishimimi no Mikoto (Tagishimimi no Mikoto)]] , * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== Other uses == It also refers to
* The Chinese transliteration of [[Mimi (TV series)]] * {{Ill|Meimei (song)|zh|美美 (歌曲)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{draft}}
{{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} is an [[honorific]] [[morpheme]] present in the names of many of the children of [[Jimmu]], and other people and things of [[Ancient japan]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> It was the title of the leader of the [[Toumakoku]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is also present in the form Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
== Overview == "Mi" (霊) is used as an honorific title (meaning a divine spirit) representing a deity, such as " '''[[Watatsumi]]''' " (sea spirit) or " [[Ōyamatsumi]] " (mountain spirit), and "Mimi" (spirit) <ref>[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>

=== More questionable overview === In the [[Kojiki]], an ancient Japanese text, [[Emperor Jimmu]] sent King Nikko-za to the Land of Dannami to slay Ku-ga-mimi Mikasa. However, this event is not mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
The Tango Fudoki Zanboku records the story of Rikumimikasa Kugamimikasa, an earth spider that was said to have been present in the Aoba Mountains during the reign of Emperor Gomajo-no-Irihiko. The people were disobedient to the emperor and King Hinokoza was sent with an imperial edict to quell the rebellion. As a result, a rocky rock resembling a golden armor was found and was called the Shogun's armor rock, also known as Narusei. The mountain Aobayama was also called Umamibajiyama, which means "horse ears" in Japanese, due to its two peaks that resemble horse ears. The two peaks have a deity named after kasa, namely [[Rokusho Shrine, Okazaki|Rokusho Shrine]] and [[Aoba Shrine]].<ref name=":0" /> The name Misukitomimikenhiko is from the Kibitakehiko of the Ki era and has meaning "noontomo" in Emperor Sotoku's stage. The emperor also frequently decreed to Yamatokenomikoto to turn the Arau Ryujin and the Maduro Hanunin of the Twelve Provinces of the East to him and make peace with them.<ref name=":0" />
The [[Fudoki]] records the story of [[Emperor Keiko]]'s accusation against Oomimi and Tarumimi of Kochinoshima. He cursed the two, whom he had never met, saying, "Even if they are put to death, it will not be enough to make up for their sins."<ref name=":0" />
== Mimi and Mi as regional chiefs == In " [[Kojiki]] " and " [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]], [[Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|" Suetsumimi]] is in [[Izumi Province|the Izumi region]], {{Lang|zh|橛}} is in [[Settsu Province|the Settsu region]], Kugamimi is in [[Tamba Province|the Tamba region]], and Maetsu is in [[Tajima Province|the Tajima region]] . An ear (Masaki tsumimi) has been recorded, but both are believed to be chiefs of their localities. In " [[Amatsuhikone|Izumo]] {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|lt=no Kuni Fudoki|de}} ", people with "mi" such as Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned, all of whom are regional chiefs. <ref>溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref>
In addition, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters ) ({{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
In addition, Ikatomi of [[Ōmi Province|Ika]] [[Ika District, Shiga|County]], Omi Province (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ) is also thought to represent a regional chief with the addition of mi to the place name. The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style posthumous name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .
== Mimi and Mi at shrines == {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]], {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]], {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in Omi Province, {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Takaichi]] County, Yamato Province, [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] [[波爾移麻比彌神社|Haniyamahimi-jinja Shrine]] in [[Mima District, Tokushima|Mima]] -gun is a shrine that has Mimi and Mi in its name.
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Representative examples of [[Uji (clan)|clan]] names ending in Mi include
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Examples of clan names that do not have mi but have mi in their [[Kamo clan|ancestors]] are
* {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kamo clan; * Deshichio's ancestor Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} 's ancestor Misuki-tomo-mimi, * [[紀氏 (神別)|Ki-shi's]] ( {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} ) ancestor Toyomimi-no-mikoto, and Hikun (Hi-kun) ), * the ancestor of the [[日置部氏|Hiokibe]] clan, Ame no Kushimi, * and the ancestor of the {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}}, Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} == Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amanoshinomimi]], as a child of [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] (also known as Hohodemi),
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== Other uses == It also refers to
* The Chinese transliteration of [[Mimi (TV series)]] * {{Ill|Meimei (song)|zh|美美 (歌曲)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{draft}}
{{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} is an [[honorific]] [[morpheme]] present in the names of many of the children of [[Jimmu]], and other people and things of [[Ancient japan]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> It was the title of the leader of the [[Toumakoku]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is also present in the form Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
== Overview == "Mi" (霊) is used as an honorific title (meaning a divine spirit) representing a deity, such as " '''[[Watatsumi]]''' " (sea spirit) or " [[Ōyamatsumi]] " (mountain spirit), and "Mimi" (spirit) <ref>[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
=== More questionable overview === In the [[Kojiki]], an ancient Japanese text, [[Emperor Jimmu]] sent King Nikko-za to the Land of Dannami to slay Ku-ga-mimi Mikasa. However, this event is not mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />

The mountain Aobayama was also called Umamibajiyama, which means "horse ears" in Japanese, due to its two peaks that resemble horse ears. The two peaks have a deity named after kasa, namely [[Rokusho Shrine, Okazaki|Rokusho Shrine]] and [[Aoba Shrine]].<ref name=":0" /> The name Misukitomimikenhiko is from the Kibitakehiko of the Ki era and has meaning "noontomo" in Emperor Sotoku's stage. The emperor also frequently decreed to Yamatokenomikoto to turn the Arau Ryujin and the Maduro Hanunin of the Twelve Provinces of the East to him and make peace with them.<ref name=":0" />
The [[Fudoki]] records the story of [[Emperor Keiko]]'s accusation against Oomimi and Tarumimi of Kochinoshima. He cursed the two, whom he had never met, saying, "Even if they are put to death, it will not be enough to make up for their sins."<ref name=":0" />

The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style posthumous name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
* {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * [[波爾移麻比彌神社|Haniyamahimi-jinja Shrine]] in [[Mima District, Tokushima|Mima]] -gun [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] is a shrine that has Mimi and Mi in its name.

* [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amanoshinomimi]], as a child of [[Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]] (also known as Hohodemi),
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").

{{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} is an [[honorific]] [[morpheme]] present in the names of many of the children of [[Jimmu]], and other people and things of [[Ancient japan]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> It was the title of the leader of the [[Toumakoku]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is also present in the form Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
== Overview == "Mi" (霊) is used as an honorific title (meaning a divine spirit) representing a deity, such as " '''[[Watatsumi]]''' " (sea spirit) or " [[Ōyamatsumi]] " (mountain spirit), and "Mimi" (spirit) <ref>[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
=== More questionable overview === In the [[Kojiki]], an ancient Japanese text, [[Emperor Jimmu]] sent King Nikko-za to the Land of Dannami to slay Ku-ga-mimi Mikasa. However, this event is not mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
The Tango Fudoki Zanboku records the story of Rikumimikasa Kugamimikasa, an earth spider that was said to have been present in the Aoba Mountains during the reign of Emperor Gomajo-no-Irihiko. The people were disobedient to the emperor and King Hinokoza was sent with an imperial edict to quell the rebellion. As a result, a rocky rock resembling a golden armor was found and was called the Shogun's armor rock, also known as Narusei. The mountain Aobayama was also called Umamibajiyama, which means "horse ears" in Japanese, due to its two peaks that resemble horse ears. The two peaks have a deity named after kasa, namely [[Rokusho Shrine, Okazaki|Rokusho Shrine]] and [[Aoba Shrine]].<ref name=":0" /> The name Misukitomimikenhiko is from the Kibitakehiko of the Ki era and has meaning "noontomo" in Emperor Sotoku's stage. The emperor also frequently decreed to Yamatokenomikoto to turn the Arau Ryujin and the Maduro Hanunin of the Twelve Provinces of the East to him and make peace with them.<ref name=":0" />
The [[Fudoki]] records the story of [[Emperor Keiko]]'s accusation against Oomimi and Tarumimi of Kochinoshima. He cursed the two, whom he had never met, saying, "Even if they are put to death, it will not be enough to make up for their sins."<ref name=":0" />
== Mimi and Mi as regional chiefs == In " [[Kojiki]] " and " [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]], [[Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|" Suetsumimi]] is in [[Izumi Province|the Izumi region]], {{Lang|zh|橛}} is in [[Settsu Province|the Settsu region]], Kugamimi is in [[Tamba Province|the Tamba region]], and Maetsu is in [[Tajima Province|the Tajima region]] . An ear (Masaki tsumimi) has been recorded, but both are believed to be chiefs of their localities. In " [[Amatsuhikone|Izumo]] {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|lt=no Kuni Fudoki|de}} ", people with "mi" such as Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned, all of whom are regional chiefs. <ref>溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref>
In addition, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters ) ({{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
In addition, Ikatomi of [[Ōmi Province|Ika]] [[Ika District, Shiga|County]], Omi Province (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ) is also thought to represent a regional chief with the addition of mi to the place name. The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style posthumous name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
* {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * [[波爾移麻比彌神社|Haniyamahimi-jinja Shrine]] in [[Mima District, Tokushima|Mima]] -gun [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] is a shrine that has Mimi and Mi in its name.
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Representative examples of [[Uji (clan)|clan]] names ending in Mi include
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Examples of clan names that do not have mi but have mi in their [[Kamo clan|ancestors]] are
* {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kamo clan; * Deshichio's ancestor Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} 's ancestor Misuki-tomo-mimi, * [[紀氏 (神別)|Ki-shi's]] ( {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} ) ancestor Toyomimi-no-mikoto, and Hikun (Hi-kun) ), * the ancestor of the [[日置部氏|Hiokibe]] clan, Ame no Kushimi, * and the ancestor of the {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}}, Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} == Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== Other uses == It also refers to
* The Chinese transliteration of [[Mimi (TV series)]] * {{Ill|Meimei (song)|zh|美美 (歌曲)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== Other uses == It also refers to
* The Chinese transliteration of [[Mimi (TV series)]] * {{Ill|Meimei (song)|zh|美美 (歌曲)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{draft}}
{{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} is an [[honorific]] [[morpheme]] present in the names of many of the children of [[Jimmu]], and other people and things of [[Ancient japan]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> It was the title of the leader of the [[Toumakoku]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is also present in the form Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
== Overview == "Mi" (霊) is used as an honorific title (meaning a divine spirit) representing a deity, such as " '''[[Watatsumi]]''' " (sea spirit) or " [[Ōyamatsumi]] " (mountain spirit), and "Mimi" (spirit) <ref>[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
=== More questionable overview === In the [[Kojiki]], an ancient Japanese text, [[Emperor Jimmu]] sent King Nikko-za to the Land of Dannami to slay Ku-ga-mimi Mikasa. However, this event is not mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
The Tango Fudoki Zanboku records the story of Rikumimikasa Kugamimikasa, an earth spider that was said to have been present in the Aoba Mountains during the reign of Emperor Gomajo-no-Irihiko. The people were disobedient to the emperor and King Hinokoza was sent with an imperial edict to quell the rebellion. As a result, a rocky rock resembling a golden armor was found and was called the Shogun's armor rock, also known as Narusei. The mountain Aobayama was also called Umamibajiyama, which means "horse ears" in Japanese, due to its two peaks that resemble horse ears. The two peaks have a deity named after kasa, namely [[Rokusho Shrine, Okazaki|Rokusho Shrine]] and [[Aoba Shrine]].<ref name=":0" /> The name Misukitomimikenhiko is from the Kibitakehiko of the Ki era and has meaning "noontomo" in Emperor Sotoku's stage. The emperor also frequently decreed to Yamatokenomikoto to turn the Arau Ryujin and the Maduro Hanunin of the Twelve Provinces of the East to him and make peace with them.<ref name=":0" />
The [[Fudoki]] records the story of [[Emperor Keiko]]'s accusation against Oomimi and Tarumimi of Kochinoshima. He cursed the two, whom he had never met, saying, "Even if they are put to death, it will not be enough to make up for their sins."<ref name=":0" />
== Mimi and Mi as regional chiefs == In " [[Kojiki]] " and " [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]], {{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}} is in [[Izumi Province|the Izumi region]], {{Lang|zh|橛}} is in [[Settsu Province|the Settsu region]], Kugamimi is in [[Tamba Province|the Tamba region]], and Maetsu is in [[Tajima Province|the Tajima region]] . An ear (Masaki tsumimi) has been recorded, but both are believed to be chiefs of their localities. In " [[Amatsuhikone|Izumo]] {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|lt=no Kuni Fudoki|de}} ", people with "mi" such as Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned, all of whom are regional chiefs. <ref>溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref>
In addition, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters ) ({{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
In addition, Ikatomi of [[Ōmi Province|Ika]] [[Ika District, Shiga|County]], Omi Province (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ) is also thought to represent a regional chief with the addition of mi to the place name. The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style posthumous name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
* {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * [[波爾移麻比彌神社|Haniyamahimi-jinja Shrine]] in [[Mima District, Tokushima|Mima]] -gun [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] is a shrine that has Mimi and Mi in its name.
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Representative examples of [[Uji (clan)|clan]] names ending in Mi include
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Examples of clan names that do not have mi but have mi in their [[Kamo clan|ancestors]] are
* {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kamo clan; * {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} ancestor Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}'s ancestor Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} ancestor Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * the ancestor of the {{ill|Hiokibe|ja|日置部氏}} clan, Ame no Kushimi, * the ancestor of the {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}}, Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}}
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== Other uses == It also refers to
* The Chinese transliteration of [[Mimi (TV series)]] * {{Ill|Meimei (song)|zh|美美 (歌曲)}}

* {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kamo clan; * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} ancestor Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}'s ancestor Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} ancestor Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * the ancestor of the {{ill|Hiokibe|ja|日置部氏}} clan, Ame no Kushimi, * the ancestor of the {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}}, Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}}, Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== Other uses == It also refers to
* The Chinese transliteration of [[Mimi (TV series)]] * {{Ill|Meimei (song)|zh|美美 (歌曲)}}

{{draft}}
{{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} is an [[honorific]] [[morpheme]] present in the names of many of the children of [[Jimmu]], and other people and things of [[Ancient japan]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> It was the title of the leader of the [[Toumakoku]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is also present in the form Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
== Overview == "Mi" (霊) is used as an honorific title (meaning a divine spirit) representing a deity, such as " '''[[Watatsumi]]''' " (sea spirit) or " [[Ōyamatsumi]] " (mountain spirit), and "Mimi" (spirit) <ref>[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
=== More questionable overview === In the [[Kojiki]], an ancient Japanese text, [[Emperor Jimmu]] sent King Nikko-za to the Land of Dannami to slay Ku-ga-mimi Mikasa. However, this event is not mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
The Tango Fudoki Zanboku records the story of Rikumimikasa Kugamimikasa, an earth spider that was said to have been present in the Aoba Mountains during the reign of Emperor Gomajo-no-Irihiko. The people were disobedient to the emperor and King Hinokoza was sent with an imperial edict to quell the rebellion. As a result, a rocky rock resembling a golden armor was found and was called the Shogun's armor rock, also known as Narusei. The mountain Aobayama was also called Umamibajiyama, which means "horse ears" in Japanese, due to its two peaks that resemble horse ears. The two peaks have a deity named after kasa, namely [[Rokusho Shrine, Okazaki|Rokusho Shrine]] and [[Aoba Shrine]].<ref name=":0" /> The name Misukitomimikenhiko is from the Kibitakehiko of the Ki era and has meaning "noontomo" in Emperor Sotoku's stage. The emperor also frequently decreed to Yamatokenomikoto to turn the Arau Ryujin and the Maduro Hanunin of the Twelve Provinces of the East to him and make peace with them.<ref name=":0" />
The [[Fudoki]] records the story of [[Emperor Keiko]]'s accusation against Oomimi and Tarumimi of Kochinoshima. He cursed the two, whom he had never met, saying, "Even if they are put to death, it will not be enough to make up for their sins."<ref name=":0" />
== Mimi and Mi as regional chiefs == In " [[Kojiki]] " and " [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]], {{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}} is in [[Izumi Province|the Izumi region]], {{Lang|zh|橛}} is in [[Settsu Province|the Settsu region]], Kugamimi is in [[Tamba Province|the Tamba region]], and Maetsu is in [[Tajima Province|the Tajima region]] . An ear (Masaki tsumimi) has been recorded, but both are believed to be chiefs of their localities. In " [[Amatsuhikone|Izumo]] {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|lt=no Kuni Fudoki|de}} ", people with "mi" such as Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned, all of whom are regional chiefs. <ref>溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref>
In addition, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters ) ({{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
In addition, Ikatomi of [[Ōmi Province|Ika]] [[Ika District, Shiga|County]], Omi Province (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ) is also thought to represent a regional chief with the addition of mi to the place name. The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style posthumous name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
* {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * [[波爾移麻比彌神社|Haniyamahimi-jinja Shrine]] in [[Mima District, Tokushima|Mima]] -gun [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] is a shrine that has Mimi and Mi in its name.
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Representative examples of [[Uji (clan)|clan]] names ending in Mi include
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Examples of clan names that do not have mi but have mi in their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] are
* {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kamo clan; * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} ancestor Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}'s ancestor Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} ancestor Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * the ancestor of the {{ill|Hiokibe|ja|日置部氏}} clan, Ame no Kushimi, * the ancestor of the {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}}, Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}}, Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== Other uses == It also refers to
* The Chinese transliteration of [[Mimi (TV series)]] * {{Ill|Meimei (song)|zh|美美 (歌曲)}}

* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * [[波爾移麻比彌神社|Haniyamahimi-jinja Shrine]] in [[Mima District, Tokushima|Mima]] -gun [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] is a shrine that has Mimi and Mi in its name.
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Representative examples of [[Uji (clan)|clan]] names ending in Mi include
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Examples of clan names that do not have mi but have mi in their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] are
* {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Kamo clan; * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} ancestor Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}'s ancestor Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} ancestor Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * the ancestor of the {{ill|Hiokibe|ja|日置部氏}} clan, Ame no Kushimi, * the ancestor of the {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}}, Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}}, Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]

{{draft}}
{{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} is an [[honorific]] [[morpheme]] present in the names of many of the children of [[Jimmu]], and other people and things of [[Ancient japan]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> It was the title of the leader of the [[Toumakoku]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is also present in the form Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
"Mi" (霊) is used as an honorific title (meaning a divine spirit) representing a deity, such as " '''[[Watatsumi]]''' " (sea spirit) or " [[Ōyamatsumi]] " (mountain spirit), and "Mimi" (spirit) <ref>[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
== Mimi and Mi as regional chiefs == In " [[Kojiki]] " and " [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]], {{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}} is in [[Izumi Province|the Izumi region]] and the [[Settsu Province|Settsu region]], Kugamimi is in [[Tamba Province|the Tamba region]], and Maetsu is in [[Tajima Province|the Tajima region]] . An ear (Masaki tsumimi) has been recorded, but both are believed to be chiefs of their localities. In " [[Amatsuhikone|Izumo]] {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|lt=no Kuni Fudoki|de}} ", people with "mi" such as Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned, all of whom are regional chiefs. <ref>溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref>
In addition, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters ) ({{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
In addition, Ikatomi of [[Ōmi Province|Ika]] [[Ika District, Shiga|County]], Omi Province (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ) is also thought to represent a regional chief with the addition of mi to the place name. The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style posthumous name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .

== References == {{Reflist}}

{{draft}}
"Mimi" and "Mi" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan, especially those of the descendants of the legendary Emperor Jimmu.<ref name=":0" /> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1" />
Spellings include {{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "Kojiki" and "Nihonshoki", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto" ruled in the Izumi and Settsu regions, "Kugamimi" ruled in the Tamba region, and "Maetsu" ruled in the Tajima region.
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the Mimi Shrine in Wakasa Province and Minakatatomi Shrine in Shinano Province.
Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, for example, the Azumi and Hozumi clans, and also in the lineage of the Imperial family, as seen in the names of some ancestors of Emperor Jimmu like Ame-no-oshihomimi and Tagishimimi.
== Overview == {{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} is an [[honorific]] [[morpheme]] present in the names of many of the children of [[Jimmu]], and other people and things of [[Ancient japan]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> It was the title of the leader of the [[Toumakoku]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is also present in the form Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
"Mi" (霊) is used as an honorific title (meaning a divine spirit) representing a deity, such as " '''[[Watatsumi]]''' " (sea spirit) or " [[Ōyamatsumi]] " (mountain spirit), and "Mimi" (spirit) <ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>

* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{draft}}
"Mimi" and "Mi" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan, especially those of the descendants of the legendary Emperor Jimmu.<ref name=":0" /> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1" />
Spellings include {{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "Kojiki" and "Nihonshoki", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto" ruled in the Izumi and Settsu regions, "Kugamimi" ruled in the Tamba region, and "Maetsu" ruled in the Tajima region.
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the Mimi Shrine in Wakasa Province and Minakatatomi Shrine in Shinano Province.
Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, for example, the Azumi and Hozumi clans, and also in the lineage of the Imperial family, as seen in the names of some ancestors of Emperor Jimmu like Ame-no-oshihomimi and Tagishimimi.
== Overview == {{nihongo|{{ill|Mimi (chief)|lt=Mimi|ja|ミミおよびミ}}|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} is an [[honorific]] [[morpheme]] present in the names of many of the children of [[Jimmu]], and other people and things of [[Ancient japan]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> It was the title of the leader of the [[Toumakoku]].<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref> It is also present in the form Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
"Mi" (霊) is used as an honorific title (meaning a divine spirit) representing a deity, such as " '''[[Watatsumi]]''' " (sea spirit) or " [[Ōyamatsumi]] " (mountain spirit), and "Mimi" (spirit) <ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
== Mimi and Mi as regional chiefs == In " [[Kojiki]] " and " [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]], {{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}} is in [[Izumi Province|the Izumi region]] and the [[Settsu Province|Settsu region]], Kugamimi is in [[Tamba Province|the Tamba region]], and Maetsu is in [[Tajima Province|the Tajima region]] . An ear (Masaki tsumimi) has been recorded, but both are believed to be chiefs of their localities. In " [[Amatsuhikone|Izumo]] {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|lt=no Kuni Fudoki|de}} ", people with "mi" such as Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned, all of whom are regional chiefs. <ref>溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref>
In addition, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the "Kojiki" (Records of Ancient Matters ) ({{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
In addition, Ikatomi of [[Ōmi Province|Ika]] [[Ika District, Shiga|County]], Omi Province (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ) is also thought to represent a regional chief with the addition of mi to the place name. The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style posthumous name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .
It was the title of the leader of [[Toumakoku]] <ref name=":02" />.
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
* {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in [[Mima District, Tokushima|Mima]] -gun [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] is a shrine that has Mimi and Mi in its name.
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Representative examples of [[Uji (clan)|clan]] names ending in Mi include
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Examples of clan names that do not have mi but have mi in their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] are
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the oldest part of [[Imperial House of Japan|the Imperial]] lineage.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]

Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
In addition, Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ) is also thought to represent a regional chief with the addition of mi to the place name. The same applies to [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] .
The leader of [[Toumakoku]] was called Mimi<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>.
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto, Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]], * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

"Mimi" and "Mi" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan, especially those of the descendants of the legendary Emperor Jimmu.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of [[Toumakoku]] was called Mimi<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>.

* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]

* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]

revision.text:
"Mimi" and "Mi" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan, especially those of the descendants of the legendary Emperor Jimmu.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of [[Toumakoku]] was called Mimi<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>.
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]

“Mimi" and "Mi" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan, especially those of the descendants of the legendary Emperor Jimmu.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, [[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Suga no Yatsumimi]], {{ill|Futimi no Kami|ja|布帝耳神}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, Tahirikishimarumi, and {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}}) is also considered a regional chief <ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref> .
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of [[Toumakoku]] was called Mimi<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>.
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}} [[ja:ミミおよびミ]][[en:Draft:Mimi and Mi]]
