Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mimi and Mi]]

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").

The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

"Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Mimi and Mi-qq]] [[fr:Mimi et Mi]] [[ja:ミミおよびミ]] [[simple:Mimi and Mi]] [[en:Mimi and Mi]]

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Kk]]
[[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Mimi and Mi-qq]] [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[fr:Mimi et Mi]] [[ja:ミミおよびミ]] [[simple:Mimi and Mi]] [[en:Mimi and Mi]]

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Kk]]
[[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Mimi and Mi-qq]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]] [[fr:Mimi et Mi]] [[ja:ミミおよびミ]] [[simple:Mimi and Mi]] [[en:Mimi and Mi]]

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Kk]]
[[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Mimi and Mi-qq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]] [[fr:Mimi et Mi]] [[ja:ミミおよびミ]] [[simple:Mimi and Mi]] [[en:Mimi and Mi]]

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").

== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

[[Category:qq-Mimi and Mi-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mimi and Mi]][[Category:qq]] [[fr:Mimi et Mi]] [[ja:ミミおよびミ]] [[simple:Mimi and Mi]] [[en:Mimi and Mi]] [[Category:ppp]]

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
