“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki Shimarumi no Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|布帝耳神" to "|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi"
revision.text:
“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[出雲国]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美は出雲国飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi

Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Ill|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Ill|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Ill|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Ill|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Ill|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Ill|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Ill|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Ill|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Ill|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. “'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]

* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").

For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. “'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

revision.text:
“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973年 - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973rd year - 1974年。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973rd year - 1974th year 。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|10=zh|11=多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==
"Mimi" and "Mi" are also present in the names of several shrines across Japan such as the {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]] and {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]. * {{ill|Mimi Shrine|ja|彌美神社}} in [[Wakasa Province]],
* {{ill|Suwa Taisha|lt=Minakatatomi Shrine|en}} in [[Shinano Province]]
* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]
== Mimi and Mi in clans == Furthermore, these words appear in the names of ancient Japanese clans, these are some of those clans.
* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).
Here are some clans without the sound in their names, but their [[Oyagami|ancestors]] have it in their names
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kamo clan|en}} - {{ill|Okamotsumi|ja|大鴨積命}} * {{ill|Achi clan|ja|阿智氏}} and {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Chichibu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|知々夫国造}} - Uma-shimi -no-mikoto, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} - Misuki-tomo-mimi, * {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造}} - Toyomimi-no-mikoto. * {{ill|Hiokibe clan|ja|日置部氏}} - Ame no Kushimi, * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nukatabe clan|ja|額田部氏}} - Ame no Yukutomi. * {{ill|Kume clan|ja|久米氏}} - Umashimimi
== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

“'''Mimi'''" and "'''Mi'''" are Japanese words that can be found in many names related to ancient Japan. They are very common in names of royal family members.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=魏志倭人伝、彌彌、彌彌那利|url=https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|access-date=2023-01-26|website=Forum_tokyoblog|archive-date=2023-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230126011011/https://tokyox.sakura.ne.jp/forum/discussion/49/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C-%E5%BD%8C%E5%BD%8C%E9%82%A3%E5%88%A9|url-status=dead}}</ref> They're also found in names of leaders, people, and objects. "Mi" can mean "divine spirit" and can be a part of names for deities. For example, the terms "[[Watatsumi]]" and "{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en}}" mean "sea spirit" and "mountain spirit" respectively and both use the version 霊.<ref name=":1">[[本居宣長]]『[[古事記伝]]』七之巻。</ref>
Spellings include {{nihongo|Mimi|ミミ、耳、彌彌、美美}} and Mi (彌、見、美、海、看).
In ancient Japanese historical texts like "[[Kojiki]]" and "[[Nihonshoki]]", people with "Mi" or "Mimi" in their names were often leaders in their regions. For instance, "{{ill|Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto|en}}" ruled in the [[Izumi Province|Izumi]] and [[Settsu Province|Settsu regions]], "Kugamimi" ruled in the [[Tamba Province|Tamba region]], and "Masakitsumimi" ruled in the [[Tajima Province|Tajima region]].<ref name=":2">溝口睦子「記紀神話解釈の一つのこころみ」『文学』1973rd year - 1974th year 。波多都美は[[Izumo Province ]][[飯石郡]][[波多郷]]の首長、支自麻都美はIzumo Province 飯石郡[[来島郷]]の首長である。</ref> and minor chiefs named Hatatsumi and Kijimatsumi are mentioned.<ref name=":2" />
More people with it in their names are, Suga no Yatsumimi, {{ill|Futemimi|ja|布帝耳神|1=Futemimi|lt=Futimi no Kami}}, {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Torimimi|ja|鳥取神}}, {{ill|Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|ja|多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami|10=zh|11=多比理岐志麻流美神|1=Tahiriki-Shimarumi-no-Kami}}, who appear in the Izumo myth of the [[Kojiki]]. {{ill|Amenohibara Oshinomi no Kami|ja|天日腹大科度美神}} is also considered a regional chief.<ref>『古事記伝』による。須賀之八耳は[[須賀郷]]ないしは[[須佐郷]]、[[美呂浪神]]は[[美呂郷]]、[[布忍富鳥鳴海神]]は[[布師郷]]のミ（首長）を表す。</ref>
Some people have names historians think came from the place they ruled, plus Mi or Mimi. People like this include Ikatomi of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Ika District, Shiga|lt=Ika District|en}}, [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] (Ikatomi, perhaps {{ill|Igatsuomi no Mikoto|ja|伊賀津臣命}} ). [[Emperor Suizei]] 's Japanese-style name, Kaunumakawamimi, and his older brother, [[Kamuyaimimi]] may come from places called Kaunumakawa and Kamuyai.
The leader of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Toumakoku|en}} was called Mimi.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=百科事典マイペディア,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,世界大百科事典|title=投馬国とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%8A%95%E9%A6%AC%E5%9B%BD-104202|access-date=2022-06-21|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref>
== Mimi and Mi at shrines ==

* {{ill|Ashizumi Shrine|ja|阿志都弥神社}} in [[Ōmi Province|Omi Province]] * {{ill|Kayanarumi Shrine|ja|加夜奈留美神社}} in Takaichi County, [[Yamato Province]] * {{ill|Haniyamahimi Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比彌神社}} in {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Mima District, Tokushima|lt=Mima District|en}} [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]]

* [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]] whose [[Oyagami]] was [[Watatsumi]] * {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} whose [[Oyagami]] was {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nigihayahi|lt=Nigihayahi|en|Amenohoakari}}. * {{ill|Wanizumi clan|ja|和珥氏}}, which is considered to be of the same family as the Wani clan * Izumozumi clan, which is considered to be of the same family as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} * Tsuzumi clan (津積氏).

* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").

== Mimi and Mi of Imperial Family Lineage == Mimi and Mi's lineage can be seen in the names of people in the Japanese royal family. Here are some of these people.
* {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en}} the grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sb3sCwAAQBAJ&q=oshihomimi&pg=PT45|title=Japanese Mythology: Hermeneutics on Scripture|last1=Isomae|first1=Jun'ichi|last2=Subramanian|first2=Mukund|date=2016-04-08|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-134-94908-3|language=en}}</ref>
* {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Tagishimimi|en}} * [[Kamuyaimimi]] * [[Emperor Suizei|Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto]] the Japanese name of Emperor Suizei * [[Hikoyai|Hikohachi Hikoyaimimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki"), * [[Kisumimi|Kisumimi no Mikoto]] (does not appear in "Nihonshoki").
=== Genealogy === Note that [[Emperor Suizei]] went by the original name of ''Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto'' {{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
